title: Constrictive pericarditis: CT diagnosis
vignette: [Item 2 of 2, same patient.] The patient is admitted to the hospital for further work-up and treatment. After initial evaluation, a CT scan of the chest is obtained and shows thickening and calcification of the pericardium. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? [Exact answer choices were off-screen in the screenshot and are reconstructed here for review.]

clues.note: Continues the same patient from item 1 (venous congestion, steep JVP descents). The CT now confirms the structural cause behind the hemodynamics.

clues.text: thickening and calcification of the pericardium
clues.note: A thickened, calcified pericardium on CT is essentially diagnostic of constrictive pericarditis. Chronic pericardial scarring (often post-TB) calcifies and forms a rigid shell that limits cardiac filling.

whatMatters: Same patient with systemic venous congestion and constrictive hemodynamics; the CT shows a THICKENED, CALCIFIED pericardium — diagnostic of constrictive pericarditis. The calcified rigid pericardium is the structural lesion behind the equalized pressures and steep y descent.
strategy: Match imaging to physiology: thickened/calcified pericardium = constrictive pericarditis; large pericardial effusion = tamponade; normal pericardium with stiff, infiltrated myocardium = restrictive cardiomyopathy. The CT finding here points straight to constriction.

eliminations.label: restrictive CM — myocardial, normal pericardium (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: restrictive cardiomyopathy stiffens the MYOCARDIUM (amyloid, sarcoid, hemochromatosis, fibrosis) with a NORMAL pericardium. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's the classic mimic of constriction hemodynamically, but the CT here shows a thickened/CALCIFIED PERICARDIUM — pointing to constriction, not a myocardial process. ✅ Correct if: imaging showed normal pericardium with infiltrated/thick myocardium and the history suggested amyloid/sarcoid.

eliminations.label: tamponade — effusion, not calcified pericardium
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: cardiac tamponade is a pericardial EFFUSION compressing the heart (acute, pulsus paradoxus, blunted y). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: CT shows pericardial THICKENING/CALCIFICATION, not fluid; the course is chronic, not acute compression. ✅ Correct if: there were a large pericardial effusion with hypotension and pulsus paradoxus.

eliminations.label: dilated CM — dilated chambers, low EF
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: dilated cardiomyopathy shows enlarged ventricles with systolic dysfunction. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the pericardial calcification and constrictive physiology don't fit a primary dilated, low-EF myocardium. ✅ Correct if: echo showed dilated chambers with globally reduced systolic function.

eliminations.label: hypertrophic CM — thick septum, not pericardium
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: HCM shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy with possible LVOT obstruction. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's a myocardial thickening problem, not a pericardial calcification; the imaging and venous-congestion picture point to constriction. ✅ Correct if: there were a thick septum, SAM, and a murmur worsening with Valsalva.

reasoning: Same patient with constrictive hemodynamics (venous congestion, steep y descent)
A rigid/calcified pericardium that limits filling = constrictive pericarditis
mechanism: Chronic pericardial inflammation (classically tuberculous, also post-surgical/radiation) leads to a fibrotic, calcified, rigid pericardium. This stiff shell prevents normal ventricular filling, producing the constrictive hemodynamics (equalized diastolic pressures, square-root sign, prominent y descent, Kussmaul sign) and systemic venous congestion. CT/MRI demonstrating pericardial thickening and calcification confirms constrictive pericarditis and distinguishes it from restrictive cardiomyopathy (normal pericardium).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Cardio > pericardial disease: constrictive pericarditis imaging (pericardial thickening/calcification on CT/MRI), causes (TB, surgery, radiation), and the constriction-vs-restriction distinction.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Let the imaging decide: a thickened/calcified PERICARDIUM = constriction (pericardial disease), not a myocardial cardiomyopathy. Pair the CT with the prior hemodynamics.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on pericardium vs myocardium, constrictive vs restrictive, calcification, tamponade? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Restrictive cardiomyopathy is the big trap (same hemodynamics) — but it has a NORMAL pericardium. The calcified pericardium on CT is what separates constriction from restriction.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The reference CT highlights a thickened, calcified pericardium. Learn to spot the bright calcified rim around the heart — the imaging signature of constrictive pericarditis.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Heads up: the answer choices for this item were off-screen in your screenshot, so I reconstructed a standard differential (constrictive vs restrictive vs tamponade, etc.). The CT finding makes constrictive pericarditis the answer — tell me if UWorld's wording differs.

keywords.back: Constriction = abnormal PERICARDIUM; restriction = abnormal MYOCARDIUM (normal pericardium)

firstAid.detail: Cardiovascular > pericardial disease: CT/MRI pericardial thickening & calcification; constriction vs restriction; etiologies and management (pericardiectomy).
firstAid.location: Cardiovascular > pericardial disease: CT/MRI pericardial thickening & calcification; constriction vs restriction; etiologies and management (pericardiectomy).

firstAid.detail: Cardiovascular > restrictive CM: infiltrative myocardial disease (amyloid, sarcoid, hemochromatosis) with normal pericardium — the main mimic.
firstAid.location: Cardiovascular > restrictive CM: infiltrative myocardial disease (amyloid, sarcoid, hemochromatosis) with normal pericardium — the main mimic.

firstAid.detail: Cardiovascular > tamponade (effusion, pulsus paradoxus), dilated and hypertrophic CM — to round out the differential.
firstAid.location: Cardiovascular > tamponade (effusion, pulsus paradoxus), dilated and hypertrophic CM — to round out the differential.

memoryHook: Calcified 'shell' around the heart on CT = constrictive pericarditis; if the pericardium is normal but the muscle is stiff, it's RESTRICTIVE instead.
